unit.name: Exposure bias, alternative training schemes, and RLHF context
unit.description: Connects training-time mismatch to generation errors and surveys methods that go beyond plain MLE.
unit.summary: The lecture revisits repetition from the training side and argues that maximum-likelihood language models can assign high probability to bad degenerate strings [ts=2473s]. The core diagnosis is exposure bias: under teacher forcing the model trains on gold prefixes, but at generation time it must condition on its own imperfect outputs, creating a harmful train-test mismatch [ts=2510s] [ts=2541s]. Scheduled sampling narrows this gap by mixing model-generated and gold tokens with a probability that increases through training, though the objective can be unstable [ts=2560s]. DAgger similarly augments training data with model-generated sequences so the model learns on states it will actually encounter [ts=2603s]. The lecture also presents retrieval-augmented generation, where the model begins from a retrieved prototype and edits it by insertion, deletion, or swapping, reducing exposure bias because generation no longer starts from scratch left-to-right [ts=2655s]. Reinforcement learning is then framed as an MDP over states, actions, policies, and rewards, with rewards derived from automatic metrics or human preference [ts=2693s] [ts=2827s]. The lecture warns that optimizing BLEU or ROUGE can game the metric rather than improve true quality [ts=2744s]. It finishes with the modern pipeline behind ChatGPT-style systems: large-scale pretraining, instruction tuning, and then RLHF, with RLHF viewed as difficult from scratch but effective on top of a strong pretrained model [ts=3110s] [ts=3130s].

key_points.text: MLE-trained language models can place high probability on degenerate repetitive strings.
Exposure bias is the mismatch between training on gold prefixes and generating from model-produced prefixes at test time.
Scheduled sampling mixes generated and gold tokens during training, gradually increasing the amount of model-generated context.
DAgger reduces train-test mismatch by adding model-generated sequences into the training distribution.
Retrieval-augmented generation starts from a retrieved prototype and edits it, reducing exposure bias.
RL for NLG treats next-token selection as actions with rewards from metrics or human preference, but optimizing proxy rewards can game the objective.
A modern ChatGPT-style pipeline is pretraining, then instruction tuning, then RLHF.

kp_catalog.name: Exposure bias
kp_catalog.description: The train-test mismatch caused by training on gold prefixes but generating on self-produced prefixes.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because exposure bias formalizes how the train-test mismatch caused by training on gold prefixes but generating on self-produced prefixes, which later units use as core machinery rather than background context.

kp_catalog.name: Training alternatives to reduce exposure bias
kp_catalog.description: Methods such as scheduled sampling, DAgger, and retrieval-augmented generation that make training conditions closer to generation conditions.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because training alternatives to reduce exposure bias clarifies methods such as scheduled sampling, DAgger, and retrieval-augmented generation that make training conditions closer to generation conditions, sharpening later modeling, analysis, or evaluation choices.

kp_catalog.name: Reinforcement learning and RLHF for NLG
kp_catalog.description: Training text generators with reward functions, including human-preference reward models layered on top of pretrained systems.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because reinforcement learning and RLHF for NLG synthesizes training text generators with reward functions, including human-preference reward models layered on top of pretrained systems into an end-to-end method or integrated recipe.
